canto_title: The Vision of the Distorted Souls and the Legend of Mantua

scenes.summary: Dante introduces the purpose of this canto, which serves to continue the story of the submerged souls.
Dante observes a crowd of souls walking through the valley with faces so twisted that they cannot see forward.
Dante describes his own intense weeping at the sight of the distorted physical forms of the souls.
Virgil reprimands Dante for his lack of fortitude and points out the tragic fate of the Theban warriors.
Virgil cites the stories of those whose forms were altered by fate, including Tiresias and the serpents.
Virgil recounts the myth of the prophetess Manto, who settled in the area that would become Mantua.
The narrative describes the waters of Lake Garda and how the Mincio river flows from it.
The text describes the creation of a swampy, uninhabited land through the flow of the river.
Virgil explains how human settlement led to the establishment of the city of Mantua upon that land.
Dante asks Virgil if he can identify any other notable or famous individuals among the passing souls.
Virgil provides a list of famous figures, including Eurypilus, Michele Scotto, and various sorceresses.
Virgil concludes his speech by warning Dante to remember these lessons as they continue their journey.
